parityMeta.describes: FIX-B (§9 canonicalKeyOrder fidelity) — synthetic objects whose sibling keys are EQUAL once lowercased (Foo/foo, AB/ab/Ab/aB, Z's Bb/bb, arr elements X/x, …). The §9 canonical byte form is the compact JSON over localeCompare-sorted keys, BYTE-IDENTICAL to src/cloudProduction/accountBoundaryLocalInventory.ts:116 stableStringify (same localeCompare key sort, same JSON.stringify key/primitive rendering, same undefined-drop). localeCompare's tertiary case tie-break sorts lower-BEFORE-upper (bar,Bar,foo,Foo — the EXACT INVERSE of the §9 canonicalKeyOrder raw code-point tie-break that shipped before FIX-B, which sorted upper-before-lower). Existing app-data goldens never carry two sibling keys equal-when-lowercased, so the canonicalKeyOrder tie-break was unexercised and could byte-drift undetected against the TS canonical-stringify; this fixture pins it. Generated from the REAL canonical-stringify (sortKeysDeep/localeCompare) + the REAL buildAppDataSnapshotHash; the Swift JSONValue.canonicalJSONString() (with canonicalKeyOrder corrected to localeCompare-faithful) is the byte-level judge — its canonical string must equal canonicalJson and its FNV-1a must equal snapshotHash, case-by-case.
parityMeta.sourceNotes: No clock — pure canonical-stringify + FNV-1a transform. Synthetic ASCII keys + integer values (the ONLY observable difference between two canonicalisations is key order, so the comparator is what is pinned). No email/token/userId. The case-fold sibling keys exercise the §9 canonicalKeyOrder case tertiary tie-break.

cases.label: top-level single-letter case-fold pair (task example) — bar<Bar, foo<Foo (lower-before-upper), bar-group before foo-group (primary b<f)
the S7 hash-fold key set (cross-consistent with program-adjust/hash-fold-cases-v1) — ab,aB,Ab,AB then zz,ZZ
nested object fold + top-level fold — inner Z{bb,bB,Bb,BB} sorts recursively, outer a<A<Z
mixed non-folding siblings interleaved with folds — primary order apple<banana<cherry preserved, only the equal-when-lowercased ties flip to lower-before-upper
array recurses into element objects — each arr element's X/x + Y/y keys sort, plus top-level M/m fold
